title: Documents Are Too Big to Embed Whole
concept: Embedding models have input limits and a single vector loses precision when it must represent an entire document.

exercises.prompt: Welcome to Unit 430! What will we explore in this unit about LLM application engineering?

options.text: How to prepare large documents for embedding and retrieval
How to train a neural network from scratch using GPUs
How to write CSS styles for an AI chat interface

exercises.explain: This unit covers Chunking, which is the foundational step of breaking down large documents so they can be processed and searched effectively by LLM applications.

cards.title: Lost in the Crowd
cards.body: When building AI search engines, we want to find the exact paragraph that answers a user's question. But if we try to convert a giant document into a single math vector, the critical details get completely lost in translation.

cards.title: The Embedding Limit
cards.body: Embedding models have hard limits on how much text they can read at once. If you feed them an entire book or a massive document, you will either hit a limit or get a single, generic vector that loses all the specific details of the text.

cards.title: The Encyclopedia Summary
cards.body: Imagine trying to summarize an entire encyclopedia volume into a single sentence. You'd lose all the specific facts! Similarly, pushing a massive document into one vector forces the model to compress too much information, wiping out the precise details.

cards.title: The Lost Clause
cards.body: If you embed a 50-page legal contract as one block, a search for a specific clause about 'late fees' will fail because that detail is drowned out by the other 49 pages of text.

cards.title: The Context Window Trap
cards.body: You might think modern LLMs with huge context windows mean we can embed whole books at once. But embedding models are different: they must squeeze everything into a single fixed-size vector, where subtle details easily get washed away.

cards.title: Blurry vs. Sharp Search
cards.body: Compare trying to search a single giant document vector versus searching multiple small chunk vectors.

left.text: One blurry, generic vector representing everything at once.

right.text: Many sharp, precise vectors pointing to exact paragraphs.

exercises.prompt: What is a primary limitation of embedding models when dealing with large documents?

options.text: They have strict input token limits and cannot accept infinite text.
They only accept documents that are exactly 100 words long.
They can only process image files, not text documents.
They require manual translation into binary code first.

exercises.explain: Embedding models have a maximum input size (token limit) and cannot process infinitely long text at once.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a document fits within an embedding model's limit, it is always best to embed it as one giant piece to keep the context intact.
exercises.explain: Even if a model doesn't throw an error, a single vector representing a huge document averages out the details, making specific search queries fail.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe what happens when we embed text that is too large.
exercises.sentence: Embedding a whole document at once makes the vector lose precision for specific topics.
exercises.explain: When we force a model to represent too much text in a single vector, it loses precision, meaning the specific details get averaged out.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following explains why a single vector cannot represent an entire large document effectively?

options.text: Large documents have a higher density of punctuation marks, which corrupts the mathematical matrix of the vector space.
Embedding models are designed to only read the first page of any document and automatically ignore subsequent pages.
A single vector has a fixed size; squeezing an entire book into it dilutes the specific meanings of individual paragraphs.

exercises.explain: Using chunks keeps the vectors focused and prevents the distinct meanings of different sections from washing each other out.

exercises.prompt: Why is it a bad idea to generate a single embedding vector for a 100-page PDF manual?

options.text: It reduces the size of the vector database without affecting search accuracy.
It exceeds model token limits and compresses too much information into one vector, losing detail.
It makes the embedding vectors too long to fit in standard database columns.
It forces the model to only read the first paragraph and ignore the rest of the file.

exercises.explain: Embedding models have strict input token limits, and jamming too much text into a single vector dilutes the specific meanings of individual paragraphs, making accurate retrieval impossible.
